title: Negation is Not Enough
summary: A deeper look at premise-negation artifacts in SNLI - and why slice-aware augmentation only goes so far.
subtitle: Diagnosing a 'premise negation → contradiction' shortcut in a fine-tuned ELECTRA-small model on SNLI, then probing how far targeted contrast sets and slice-aware loss reweighting can push it.

cardImage.alt: Negation patterns and accuracy on SNLI

heroImage.alt: Premise vs hypothesis negation accuracy on SNLI

sections.title: Overview
sections.body:
State-of-the-art NLI models routinely lean on shallow lexical heuristics - antonyms, bag-of-words overlap, and the presence of a single negation word - to clear benchmarks like SNLI. This project asks a narrower question: where in a sentence does negation appear, and does that location change how the model behaves?

I fine-tuned a baseline ELECTRA-small discriminator on SNLI, then sliced its predictions along three axes: (i) part-of-speech region of the negation cue, (ii) lexical overlap between premise and hypothesis, and (iii) whether negation appears in the premise, hypothesis, both, or neither. The findings motivated a two-stage intervention - contrast-set augmentation plus slice-aware loss reweighting - explicitly designed to keep the model architecture frozen and probe how far data-side fixes can take you.

sections.title: Headline findings
sections.intro: Three results drove the rest of the project:
items: Premise negation is vanishingly rare in SNLI. Only ~0.6% of validation examples contain a negation cue in the premise - captions from Flickr30k almost never assert what is not happening.

The model overgeneralizes 'negation → contradiction', but only on the premise side. Hypothesis-negation reflects a real dataset prior (NRI ≈ 0.025); premise-negation injects an unwarranted bias (NRI ≈ 0.099) where gold labels don't actually skew toward contradiction.

Augmentation helps coverage but not the core artifact. Adding contrast examples and reweighting slices improved overall accuracy modestly (85.4% → 86.2%) and lifted rare slices, yet premise-negation NRI for contradiction increased to 0.147 - the shortcut is structural, not just under-sampled.

sections.title: NPAS: locating negation in the dependency tree
sections.body:
To analyze where negation operates, I built a small pipeline I call NPAS (Negation-Perturbation Analysis with Slices). For each premise/hypothesis it runs a spaCy parse, detects cues using a layered approach - single-token cues (not, n't, never, no), negative pronouns (nobody, nothing), negative verbs (deny, refuse, lack, fail), the without preposition, the rule out particle verb, and multi-token Matcher patterns like never ever and in no way, plus a neither…nor sentence-level rule - and then walks each cue's anchor token to classify it into one of seven syntactic regions.

The regions are root (main predicate), subject, object, locative, attribute, modal, and quantifier. Each cue gets a scope span via the anchor's subtree, and pair-level features encode which regions are negated in the premise vs. hypothesis. This is what made it possible to ask questions like 'how does the model do when negation modifies the subject of the premise?' rather than just 'is there a not somewhere?'.

sections.title: Baseline accuracy by negation pattern
sections.alt: Table showing accuracy across negation patterns and lexical overlap buckets
sections.caption: Out of 10,000 SNLI validation examples, only 208 contain explicit negation in the premise. Accuracy on premise-negation examples drops to 75.4% - well below the 85.4% overall baseline.

sections.title: Negation Reliance Index
sections.body:
To quantify how much the model overuses negation as a cue, I defined a Negation Reliance Index (NRI):

NRI(ℓ) = Δ_pred(ℓ) - Δ_gold(ℓ)

where Δ is the change in label probability conditioned on negation being present vs. absent. NRI > 0 means the model leans on negation as evidence for label ℓ more strongly than the dataset itself does. It separates real dataset bias (which the model is supposed to learn) from spurious model bias (which it shouldn't).

sections.title: NRI for CONTRADICTION: hypothesis vs. premise
sections.alt: NRI table comparing hypothesis-negation and premise-negation reliance for the contradiction label
sections.caption: Hypothesis-negation NRI is small (0.025) - the model mirrors a real dataset coupling. Premise-negation NRI is four times larger (0.099) and points the wrong way: gold labels for premise-negation are roughly balanced, yet the model predicts contradiction 40% of the time.

sections.title: Intervention: contrast sets + slice-aware reweighting
sections.body:
The intervention had two stages. First, I hand-built ~150 contrastive examples seeded from SNLI premises with explicit negation, generating hypotheses that (i) preserve the core event, (ii) cover under-represented region patterns, and (iii) deliberately produce NEUTRAL or ENTAILMENT labels - directly attacking the 'premise negation = contradiction' shortcut. Examples were split 70/30 into train and validation after a deterministic shuffle.

Second, after a dry training run to recompute slice error rates, I applied a margin-dependent reweighting in the cross-entropy loss: examples in slices where the baseline performed worse than overall accuracy (premise root, premise subject, premise quantifier) were upweighted; slices the baseline already handled well were not. Architecture, optimizer, and hyperparameters stayed identical to the baseline.

sections.title: Results: baseline vs. augmented
sections.alt: Comparison of baseline and augmented model accuracy across negation patterns
sections.caption: Augmentation improved overall accuracy (85.4% → 86.2%) and dramatically improved the BOTH slice (50% → 85%), but PREM_ONLY accuracy fell from 75.7% to 66.7% on a larger, harder evaluation slice.

sections.title: Takeaways
items: Slice-aware augmentation can stabilize aggregate accuracy while exposing rare patterns - the augmented model didn't regress on the easy majority slice, which isn't guaranteed.
Lexical overlap is not where the model fails most. The baseline handled high-overlap examples (92%) better than low-overlap (85%). The interesting failure mode is structural, not surface.

'Hypothesis-negation → contradiction' is mostly a real dataset prior. The model isn't inventing it; it's learning what SNLI teaches. NRI is a useful tool for telling those cases apart from genuinely spurious shortcuts.

Premise-negation is the spurious one - and dataset-side fixes don't fully resolve it. Even after augmentation and reweighting, premise-root and premise-subject negation accuracy stayed in the 58–59% range. Architectural interventions (residual debiasing heads, ensemble-based artifact experts) are likely needed for real movement here.
Building NPAS was 60% of the value. Once you can ask 'where does negation live?' as a structured query rather than a regex, every other diagnostic in the project comes nearly free.

sections.title: Limitations and what I'd do next
sections.body:
Three honest caveats. First, every result is tied to SNLI; the contrast sets are small and adversarial by construction, not a sample of natural premise-negation. Second, NPAS region assignment relies on spaCy's parser plus hand-written rules - parser errors and dependency-vs-semantic-scope mismatches are the dominant noise source, and complex sentences are likely mis-categorized at non-trivial rates. Third, the project deliberately froze the model architecture to isolate dataset-side effects; prior work (residual correction heads, artifact-expert ensembles, debiased fine-tuning) suggests the ceiling on premise-negation slices is much higher with model-side interventions.

If I picked this up again I'd swap the rule-based scope detection for a learned negation-scope model, scale the contrast set with templated generation rather than hand-editing, and pair the reweighting scheme with a small residual debiasing head - partly to test whether NRI on premise-negation can actually be driven to zero, and partly because the diagnostic toolkit deserves a stronger model to evaluate.
